summary: The data industry is regressing despite having excellent tools because practitioners lack foundational knowledge in areas like data modeling, normalization, and data warehousing principles. Rather than studying established practices from industry pioneers like Kimball and Inmon, many rely on hearsay, AI-generated answers, and tool-specific knowledge while skipping fundamentals. The solution requires both company investment in employee development and individual practitioners taking ownership of their professional growth.
key_points: Data modeling knowledge is critically lacking - many practitioners confuse dbt models with actual data modeling principles
The 'One Big Table' anti-pattern stems from avoiding joins without understanding the tradeoffs
People denormalize data without first understanding normalization forms
Data warehousing is confused with technology rather than understood as a methodology for supporting decision-making
Tools have a ~2 year shelf life but fundamentals follow the Lindy Effect and last much longer
Companies must invest in training while practitioners must take charge of their own education
money_quotes: If you mention data modeling to the latest crop of practitioners, they might assume you're talking about dbt models instead of the practice and craft of data modeling.
It's almost impossible to master your craft if you're not studying the giants of our industry.
Better tools mean you can ship crappier things faster.
Do you want to know the brand of scalpel you'll use for open heart surgery or how the heart works?
Despite technological advances, the industry will continue to slide backward until our skills and knowledge can rise to the occasion.
key_insight: The data industry's obsession with new tools over foundational knowledge is causing it to regress, and only a renewed focus on time-tested principles from industry pioneers can reverse this decline.
